---
date: "2026-06-22"
date-format: "YYYY-MM-DD"
---

## Robustness & Inference Stress-Tests: Kill Your Own Result

**Learning objectives.** By the end you can:

- Re-run standard errors under every defensible clustering level
- Build placebo tests where no effect can exist
- Correct a family of outcomes with Bonferroni or Benjamini–Hochberg
- Bound an unobserved confounder with Oster's $\delta$

::: notes
Welcome to Week Eight, Chapter eight-point-two, part one of eight. Last chapter you wrapped your pre-registered result in a specification curve — every defensible analytic choice, plotted, so a reader sees your headline number is not one lucky fork in the garden. That was about analytic-choice multiplicity: the many regressions you could have run. This chapter is more adversarial. Here you stop varying your choices and start attacking your inference itself. This opening part frames the lesson and sets up the worked example we will hunt all the way through.
:::

## From Stable Choices to Attacking Inference

- Last chapter: *is my estimate stable across specifications?*
- This chapter: *is my estimate even real?*
- Threats: correlated errors, spurious patterns, tuned knobs
- Also: a family tested at once, an unmeasured confounder
- The ugly-named, beautiful goal: **kill your own result**
- Run a hostile referee's tests on yourself first

::: notes
The question last chapter was: is my estimate stable across reasonable specifications? This chapter asks something sharper: is my estimate even real, or have I been fooled? Fooled by correlated errors that make my t-statistic lie. By a pattern that would show up even where no treatment happened. By a knob I tuned until the stars appeared. By a family of outcomes I tested all at once. Or by a confounder I never measured. The discipline here has an ugly name and a beautiful purpose: you are going to try to kill your own result. Not soften it, not hedge it — kill it. You design the tests a hostile referee would design, run them on yourself first, and report what they find whether or not you like the answer. This is the single most important professional habit in empirical work, and it is what separates a paper a reader trusts from one they merely read. The reveal-the-trick promise of this book turns, one last time, on you: the trick you are now revealing is the one you might be playing on yourself.
:::

## Column 3 Comes Due

- Chapter 7.5: an identification memo, threats-and-responses table
- Column three was *"what we do"* — a set of promises
- This chapter is where those promises come due
- Each test operationalizes one row's column three
- Placebo, wild cluster bootstrap, Oster delta — each an answer owed
- Keep your own threats table open as you watch

::: notes
Here is the spine, so you have it before the machinery. In Chapter seven-point-five you wrote an identification memo with a threats-and-responses table — four columns, the third of which was "what we do about it." Column three was a set of promises. This chapter is where those promises come due. Each robustness test below is the operationalization of one row's column three: a placebo for the "this pattern is spurious" threat, a wild cluster bootstrap for the "few clusters break my standard errors" threat, an Oster delta for the "unobserved confounder" threat. So as you read, keep your own threats table open. Every test here is an answer you owe to a row you already wrote.
:::

## The Running Example: Maya's Fair-Lending Study

- Staggered difference-in-differences on H-M-D-A mortgage data
- Did state fair-lending exams shrink the denial gap?
- Estimator: Callaway–Sant'Anna overall A-T-T, clustered by state
- Result: A-T-T of about $-1.4$ percentage points
- State-clustered standard error about $0.55$, t about $2.5$
- Three stars, a good day — now make it die

::: notes
We continue with Maya's project from Week Seven: a staggered difference-in-differences on H-M-D-A mortgage data, asking whether state fair-lending examination programs reduced the county-level minority-white gap in mortgage-denial rates. Her identifying assumption is per-cohort parallel trends; her primary estimate is a Callaway–Sant'Anna overall average treatment effect on the treated, against never- and not-yet-treated controls, clustered by state. Suppose she ran it and got an A-T-T of roughly negative one-point-four percentage points — the examination programs appear to shrink the denial gap by one-point-four points — with a state-clustered standard error of about zero-point-five-five, so a t of about two-point-five and three stars on the table. A good day. Now we spend the chapter trying to make that number die.
:::

## Key Takeaway

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
Robustness is not soft hedging — it is **trying to kill your own result** the way a hostile referee would, and reporting what you find. Every stress-test answers one row of your Chapter 7.5 threats table. Next: the cheapest attack — standard errors.
:::

::: notes
To summarize part one: this chapter is about adversarial inference, not analytic-choice multiplicity. You design the attacks a hostile referee would run, you run them on yourself first, and you report the verdict honestly. Every test operationalizes one promise from column three of your Chapter seven-point-five threats table. We will hunt Maya's negative one-point-four percentage-point estimate, with its t of two-point-five, all the way through. In part two we start with the cheapest and most common attack: the standard error that is too small, the t-statistic that is too big, and the significance that is an illusion.
:::
